satisfaction.reasoning: The draft is structurally solid and avoids condescension, but it misses several hidden expectations: no architectural decisions include rejected alternatives (H1 scores 0), the 'Things That Will Bite You' section appears after the code sections where someone would already have hit those problems (H3 scores 0), code examples are missing the required one-line 'what this does' comment and some exceed 15 lines without the prose earning it (H2 scores 0.5), and the headers read more like labeled sections than a narrative tutorial (H4 scores 0.5).

H1 and H5 are solid, and the architectural reasoning is genuinely useful now. The main issue is that pitfalls and warnings are still collected at the end under 'Things That Will Bite You' — which means someone reading linearly will hit the refresh token reuse problem before they see the warning about it. H2 also has a gap: the code examples are minimal but none of them have the required one-line 'what this does' comment above them.
